skill.name: wrangler
skill.description: Use Wrangler CLI commands to manage Cloudflare Workers, Pages, KV, R2, D1, secrets, authentication, and deployments.
skill.summary: Cloudflare Wrangler command guide for deployment, storage, databases, secrets, and account checks.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: The static Ruby backtick and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline backticks, and Cloudflare product names. The skill is a legitimate Wrangler CLI reference, but many documented commands can change Cloudflare production resources, query data, upload files, deploy applications, or set secrets when used with an authenticated account.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Cloudflare Resource Modification Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents Wrangler commands that deploy Workers, modify KV values, upload R2 objects, run D1 migrations, deploy Pages directories, and set secrets. These are legitimate Cloudflare operations, but they can affect production resources if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Wrangler operations that can write, deploy, upload, migrate, or set secrets. They are normal for a Wrangler skill, so the concern is operational impact rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Secret Handling Through Shell Pipeline
medium_findings.description: The documented secret example pipes a placeholder secret value into Wrangler. This avoids storing the value in the skill, but users could expose real secrets through shell history, terminal logs, or copied prompts if they paste sensitive values directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example is a standard Wrangler workflow and uses a placeholder value. The risk depends on how users substitute real secrets in their local shell.

low_findings.title: Cloudflare Resource Enumeration Commands
low_findings.description: The skill includes commands that list deployments, KV namespaces, KV keys, R2 buckets, D1 databases, Pages projects, secrets, and the current Cloudflare identity. These commands are legitimate but may reveal account metadata to anyone with terminal access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples clearly enumerate Cloudflare resources and identity information. No evidence shows exfiltration or unauthorized access beyond normal authenticated CLI output.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Pattern: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The external command alerts for Ruby or shell backtick execution are false positives. SKILL.md contains Markdown inline code and fenced command examples, not Ruby source or executable script logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown documentation with code fences. There is no Ruby file, shell script, or automatic execution wrapper in the analyzed file.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Pattern: Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at the description line is a false positive. The text references Cloudflare product names such as R2 and D1, not cryptographic functions or hashing algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is front matter description text and contains no cryptographic operation. The finding appears to come from matching product names rather than code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wrangler Commands Can Mutate Remote Services
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides CLI examples for deployment, key-value writes, object uploads, database migrations, Pages deploys, and secret updates. Users should review targets and credentials before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the skill file and map directly to remote Cloudflare changes. The pattern is expected for this skill but still requires user review.

content.value_statement: Cloudflare teams often need quick recall of Wrangler commands across Workers, Pages, KV, R2, D1, and secrets. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concise command guide for common Cloudflare operations.
actual_capabilities: Shows Wrangler commands for listing deployments and deploying Workers.
Provides KV namespace, key listing, read, and write command examples.
Provides R2 bucket listing, object listing, and file upload examples.
Shows D1 database listing, SQL execution, and migration commands.
Includes Pages deployment, secret management, authentication check, and login commands.
limitations: Does not validate Cloudflare account permissions or Wrangler authentication state.
Does not generate full project configuration files or worker source code.
Does not explain every Wrangler option, flag, or service-specific setting.
Does not prevent users from running commands against production resources.

use_cases.title: Deploy a Worker
use_cases.description: Find the basic Wrangler commands needed to inspect deployments, run locally, tail logs, and deploy a Worker.

use_cases.title: Inspect Cloudflare Storage
use_cases.description: Look up commands for KV namespaces, KV keys, R2 buckets, R2 objects, and D1 databases.

use_cases.title: Manage Release Operations
use_cases.description: Use a compact checklist for Pages deploys, D1 migrations, secrets, login, and account identity checks.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Wrangler Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Which Wrangler command should I use to list my Cloudflare Workers deployments?

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Local Worker Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the Wrangler commands to run a Worker locally, tail its logs, and deploy it after testing.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Storage Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to inspect KV keys and upload one file to R2. Show the relevant Wrangler commands and note what I must verify first.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Release Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe Wrangler release checklist for a Pages deploy, D1 migration, and secret update across staging and production.

output_examples.input: I need to deploy a Worker and watch logs.
output_examples.output: Use the local development command first, deploy after review, then tail the named Worker for live logs. Confirm the target account before deploying.

output_examples.input: How do I inspect Cloudflare storage?
output_examples.output: Start by listing KV namespaces, R2 buckets, and D1 databases. Then inspect keys, objects, or database rows only in the intended environment.

output_examples.input: I need to update a secret.
output_examples.output: Check the current Cloudflare identity, select the correct Worker, and use Wrangler secret management. Avoid pasting real secrets into shared logs.

best_practices: Confirm the Cloudflare account, project, and environment before running any write command.
Use staging resources before production deployments, migrations, and storage updates.
Keep secrets out of prompts, shared terminals, screenshots, and persistent shell history.
anti_patterns: Running deployment or migration commands without checking the active Cloudflare account.
Pasting real secret values into chat messages, tickets, or shared terminal sessions.
Using placeholder resource names without replacing them with verified project identifiers.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps recall common Wrangler CLI commands for Cloudflare Workers, Pages, KV, R2, D1, secrets, and login tasks.

faq.question: Does it run Wrangler commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command guidance. The user or agent must decide whether to run commands in the local environment.

faq.question: Can it deploy production Cloudflare resources?
faq.answer: The documented commands can deploy or modify production resources if used with production credentials and targets.

faq.question: Does it include security warnings?
faq.answer: The skill itself is brief, so users should add their own review steps before deployments, migrations, uploads, and secret changes.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I verify before using it?
faq.answer: Verify Wrangler authentication, the active Cloudflare account, resource names, project names, and the target environment.
